methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Growth → next-stage step-up multiplier (1.2x) to $703M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Defense & Security (found 0 same-stage, 0 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 19 same-sector peers exist in Defense & Security, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

public.rationale: Public-co frontier-tech comp in Defense & Dual-use
Public-co frontier-tech comp in Defense & Dual-use

data.narrative:
Improbable Defence is a Growth-stage defense and security company that has raised $703M to date. The valuation exercise draws on a comp universe of 10 peers across the sector—2 public (DRSHF, IDEAFORGE.NS) and 8 private firms including Anduril Industries, Helsing, and Shield AI—though narrow-filter peer matching proved limited.

A single applicable method anchors this analysis: Stage-Progression, which applies a 1.2x step-up multiplier typical of Growth-stage advancement to the $703M total-raised proxy, yielding $844M. A traditional Peer-Median approach was not deployable; while 19 same-sector comparables exist, filtering to same-stage, adjacent-stage, or similar-valuation-tier peers yielded fewer than three datapoints, making sector-wide median comparison unreliable.

The triangulation supports a valuation range of $717M to $970M, with a median of $844M. This reflects meaningful uncertainty: the single-method reliance leaves the analysis vulnerable to the appropriateness of the 1.2x multiplier and the $703M proxy assumption. Key uncertainties include the absence of tightly-matched public comps at equivalent stage and the sparsity of private peer financials at Improbable Defence's apparent scale. Investors should treat the midpoint as indicative pending access to more recent growth and profitability metrics from comparable-stage defence-tech entrants.
